behaviors.description: A plugin whose manifest is missing a required field is rejected before any code from that plugin is executed.
behaviors.justification: The behavior specifies code must not be executed. A unit test with mocked require() wouldn't catch the real failure mode: validation passes but the loader imports the module anyway. To verify execution doesn't happen, we need real plugin files with observable import-time side effects (e.g., setting a flag, throwing, or initializing shared state). Only then can we confirm the module was never imported.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real plugin directory with a manifest.json missing a required field. The plugin's entry module sets a global flag or counter during import. Run the loader on this directory. Assert the loader reports a validation error before the import-time side effect occurs (flag remains unset). Verify neither the plugin code nor its onLoad hook are executed.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test requires real files + observable import-time behavior to verify the plugin is not imported, which is Integration level.

behaviors.description: When plugin A declares a dependency on plugin B, plugin B's onLoad hook is called before plugin A's.
behaviors.justification: The behavior is specifically about the invocation order of onLoad hooks. Mocked plugins with spied onLoad functions are sufficient to catch the failure modes: hook not called, hook called in wrong order, or hook skipped. The dependency resolution and load order will naturally determine call sequence. No import-time semantics or real files needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create two mock plugins: A declares a dependency on B. Both have mocked onLoad hooks. Run the loader. Assert that B's onLoad is called exactly once, A's onLoad is called exactly once, and B's call occurs strictly before A's call (verified via mock call order).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mocked onLoad hooks with call-order tracking are sufficient. No real files, I/O, or import semantics needed—this is Unit level.

behaviors.description: When two plugins each declare a dependency on the other, the loader reports the cycle and loads neither.
behaviors.justification: The behavior has two parts: reporting an error and preventing load. Reporting can be Unit (mock, check error thrown), but verifying 'neither loads' requires real files. A unit test mocking onLoad hooks wouldn't catch the real failure: cycle detection works but loader still imports both modules anyway. We need observable evidence that the plugin code was never executed.
behaviors.test_description: Create two real plugin files with circular dependencies (A→B, B→A). Each entry module sets a flag on import. Run the loader. Assert the loader reports a dependency cycle error AND neither plugin's import-time side effect occurs (both flags remain unset). Verify no onLoad hooks are called.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: To verify 'neither loads', we need real plugin files with observable import behavior, not mocked onLoad. This requires Integration.

behaviors.description: A plugin's onLoad hook is only invoked after the plugin's entry module has been imported and executed.
behaviors.justification: The behavior specifies a temporal constraint: import-time execution must complete before onLoad is called. This is not just about the import statement but about the module's initialization side effects. Mocking require() is fragile in Node.js (requires proxyquire or monkey-patching Module). The real failure is: onLoad is called before the module's initialization logic runs. We need a real module with observable import-time behavior (initializing state that onLoad depends on) to verify the order.
behaviors.test_description: Create a plugin entry module that initializes a global variable during import (before exporting onLoad). Implement onLoad to assert this variable is set. Run the loader on this plugin. Assert the loader imports the module first, then calls onLoad. The test passes if onLoad's assertion succeeds (module was imported before hook was called).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Verifying import-time side effects and their order relative to onLoad requires real plugin files, not mocks. This is Integration.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error during its onLoad hook, the loader continues initializing the remaining plugins that do not depend on it.
behaviors.justification: The behavior is about loader control flow: which plugins are initialized after one fails. We can verify this with mocked plugins by making one plugin's onLoad throw, then checking which mocked onLoad hooks are called. This catches the real failure modes: error handling that crashes the whole load sequence, or incorrectly skips or loads plugins due to bad dependency logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create three mock plugins: A (onLoad throws error), B (depends on A), C (independent). Run the loader. Assert that A's onLoad is called and throws, B's onLoad is never called (skipped due to A's failure), and C's onLoad is called exactly once. Verify the loader handles the error gracefully without crashing.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test only needs to verify control flow (which onLoad is called, which is skipped), which mocks provide via call tracking. No real files or import semantics needed—this is Unit.
